question_text: Explain why a planet with twice the mass of Earth, but the same radius, would have a stronger gravitational field than Earth.
correct_answer: Gravitational field strength depends on the mass of the object creating the field and the distance from its centre. With twice the mass but the same radius, the stronger gravitational force at the surface means a stronger gravitational field.
explanation: Gravitational field strength increases with the mass of the object creating it (proportional to mass) and decreases with distance squared. Doubling the mass while keeping radius the same doubles the field strength at the surface.
